blocks.heading: Let's remember spreadsheet basics. 📊
blocks.html:
You learned this last year. Let's go over it fast.

A spreadsheet stores data in a grid of rows and columns.

blocks.heading: Rows go across. Columns go up and down. ⬛
blocks.html:
Rows are numbered: 1, 2, 3...
Columns are labelled: A, B, C...

blocks.heading: A cell is one box. A worksheet is one page. 📦
blocks.html:
Cell A2 is in column A, row 2.

A worksheet is one page in a spreadsheet file. You can have many worksheets.

blocks.heading: Formulas calculate. Functions are shortcuts. 🧮
blocks.html:
A formula always starts with =.

Example: =A1+B1 adds two cells.

SUM and AVERAGE are functions. They do the maths for you.

blocks.title: Match each word to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: Goes up and down the page ⬇️
One single box in the grid 📦
A shortcut that does maths for you ⚙️

question.text: What must every formula start with? 🧮
question.explanation: Every formula starts with the = sign. This tells the spreadsheet to calculate.

question.text: Which function adds up a group of cells? ➕
question.explanation: SUM adds up all the values in a range of cells, like =SUM(B2:B6).
Cell addresses are column first, then row. So B3 = column B, row 3.

blocks.heading: Percentage shows how big a part is. 💯
blocks.html:
A percentage compares a part to a whole.

It is shown with a % sign. Example: 50%.

blocks.heading: You can turn a number into a percentage. 🔢
blocks.html:
Divide the part by the whole. Then multiply by 100.

Example: 20 out of 40 is 50%.

blocks.heading: A percentage formula uses division. ➗
blocks.html:
Example: =B2/C2

If B2 = 20 and C2 = 40, the answer is 0.5.

blocks.heading: Set the cell format to Percentage. 🏷️
blocks.html:
The spreadsheet then shows your answer as a % automatically.

0.5 becomes 50% on the screen.

blocks.heading: Percentages help you compare fairly. ⚖️
blocks.html:
They show a share out of 100, no matter how big the totals are.

This makes results easy to compare.

blocks.title: Complete this formula to find what percentage B2 is of C2: =___
blocks.explanation: Divide the part (B2) by the whole (C2). Then format the cell as a percentage.

question.explanation: A percentage shows a part compared to a whole, out of 100. It uses the % sign.

question.text: Which formula works out a percentage? 🧮
question.explanation: Divide the part by the whole to get a percentage. Example: =B2/C2.
20 divided by 40 is 0.5. As a percentage, that is 50%.

blocks.heading: Different charts show data in different ways. 📊
blocks.html:
You learned about charts last year.

Now let's learn which chart to pick for the job.

blocks.heading: A column chart compares amounts. 📊
blocks.html:
It uses tall bars standing up.

Good for comparing groups, like sales by month.

blocks.heading: A bar chart compares amounts too. 📊
blocks.html:
It uses bars lying on their side.

Good for comparing items with long names.

blocks.heading: A pie chart shows parts of a whole. 🥧
blocks.html:
Each slice is a percentage of the total.

All slices together always make 100%.

blocks.heading: A line chart shows change over time. 📈
blocks.html:
It uses dots joined by a line.

Good for showing trends, like temperature over a week.

blocks.heading: Pick the chart that fits your data. ✅
blocks.html:
Comparing groups? Use column or bar.
Showing parts of 100%? Use pie.
Showing change over time? Use line.

blocks.title: Match each chart type to what it is best for. 🧩

question.text: Which chart is best to show parts of a whole? 🥧
question.explanation: A pie chart shows percentages of a whole. All the slices together make 100%.

question.text: Which chart is best to show change over time? 📈
question.explanation: A line chart uses dots joined by a line. This makes it easy to see a trend over time.

question.text: What is the difference between a column chart and a bar chart? 📊
options: Column bars stand up, bar chart bars lie sideways
A bar chart can only show one value
question.explanation: Column charts use upright bars. Bar charts use sideways bars. Both compare amounts.

blocks.heading: Spreadsheets need good design too. 🎨
blocks.html:
Good design helps your audience understand the data.

Your audience is the people who will read it.

blocks.heading: Use colour with a clear purpose. 🌈
blocks.html:
Use colour to highlight important cells, like the header row.

Too many colours make a spreadsheet confusing.

blocks.heading: Readability means easy to read. 👀
blocks.html:
Use a clear font and a sensible size.

Leave space between columns. Don't cram in too much.

blocks.heading: Charts must also be easy to read. 📊
blocks.html:
Always add a title to your chart.

Label each axis so people know what the numbers mean.

blocks.heading: Pick design choices for your audience. 🎯
blocks.html:
A spreadsheet for young children might use bright colours and pictures.

A spreadsheet for a business report should look neat and simple.

blocks.title: Put these design steps in the best order. 🎨
orderedItems: Choose a clear font and size
Add colour to the header row only
Add a title and axis labels to the chart

question.text: Why should you think about your audience first? 🎯
options: It changes how you should design the spreadsheet
It does not matter for spreadsheets
question.explanation: Your audience affects your design choices, like colour, font size, and layout.

question.text: What should every chart have to help readers understand it? 📊
question.explanation: A title tells readers what the chart shows. Labelled axes explain what the numbers mean.

question.text: How much colour should you use in a spreadsheet? 🌈
options: Only where it helps, like the header row
question.explanation: Use colour with a clear purpose, such as highlighting headers. Too much colour makes data hard to read.
